Facility Name: University of Tennessee Anthropological Research Facility (UTK ARF / FAC “Body Farm”)
Associated University/Entity: University of Tennessee (Forensic Anthropology Center - FAC)
History of Directors: William M. Bass (founder); Dawnie Wolfe Steadman (Director Emeritus); Giovanna Vidoli (current)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Largest program; 100+ donations/year historically; >1,000 individuals in Donated Skeletal Collection. Diverse demographics documented in publications.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Pioneering PMI estimation, entomology, scavenging, microbial ecology (DeBruyn), FORDISC software. Hundreds of papers; foundational for global network.
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate humid subtropical/continental; varied microenvironments (surface, buried, clothed, vehicles, submerged)
Unique Features/Notes: No public tours; major LE training; new 2026 lab triples casework capacity. World-renowned Bass Skeletal Collection. Rejects certain infections.
Other Helpful Data: Iconic first facility. Books: 'Death's Acre'. Strong ethics focus. Ongoing taphonomy, ID, trauma research.

Current Director: Angela Dautartas, PhD (Director of Forensic Anthropology Facilities)
History of Directors: Cheryl A. Johnston, PhD (founding/longtime Director; now NAGPRA Coordinator)
Donor Packet/Info URL: Contact Dr. Angela Dautartas (adautartas@wcu.edu) for packets
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Smaller scale; willed donors curated into documented John A. Williams Human Skeletal Collection. Quality-focused for research/training.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Taphonomy in temperate mountains; elevation/vegetation/scavenging effects; K9 training; fire/thermal bone alteration; surface recovery. Multiple publications.
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate mountain (cooler, higher precip, seasonal); Blue Ridge province
Unique Features/Notes: Strong student experiential learning & LE training (cadaver dogs). Permanent skeletal curation. Ethical willed-body program.
Other Helpful Data: One of smallest but highly active for training. Part of broader WCU forensic facilities.

Associated University/Entity: Texas State University (Forensic Anthropology Center at Texas State)
Current Director: Dr. Kate Spradley / FACTS faculty team (check site)
History of Directors: Founding: Dr. Michelle Hamilton, Dr. Jerry Melbye; ongoing FACTS researchers
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Regular donations (hospitals, ME, funeral homes). Large research output; Texas-region focus.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Hot semi-arid decomposition; vulture scavenging (GPS/thermal tracking); PMI models; clandestine grave detection; drone/IR; trauma/taphonomic alterations. Many NIJ projects.
Size/Acreage: ~26 acres (largest; part of 4,200+ acre Freeman Ranch)
Climate/Environment Notes: Hot semi-arid / subtropical; high temps, low humidity, intense sun, dominant vulture activity
Unique Features/Notes: Largest outdoor human decomposition facility. Unique vulture research. Educational outreach + LE training on working ranch with varied topography.
Other Helpful Data: Emphasizes real-world Texas/Mexico border forensics. Strong remote sensing/GIS for body recovery.

Facility Name: Southeast Texas Applied Forensic Science Facility (STAFS)
History of Directors: Associated with SHSU Forensic Science Dept (early involvement by Dr. Joan Bytheway et al.)
Donor Packet/Info URL: Contact SHSU Forensic Science (Anatomical Board of Texas recognized)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Willed-body donor facility; supports student/LE training in humid environment. Limited public aggregate metrics.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Humid subtropical forest decomposition; clandestine grave research (burial detection, geophysics, remote sensing); LE training exercises; morgue facilities.
Climate/Environment Notes: Humid subtropical (hot, humid, high rainfall, dense pineywoods forest)
Unique Features/Notes: Emphasizes realistic clandestine disposal scenarios + training. Webcams for monitoring. Complements drier FARF site.
Contact Info: SHSU Criminal Justice / Forensic Science Dept
Other Helpful Data: One of two major Texas facilities. Good for insect activity in humid conditions.

Current Director: Gretchen R. Dabbs, PhD & D.C. Martin (founders)
History of Directors: Founders: Gretchen R. Dabbs, D.C. Martin (2010 onward)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Initially pig-focused; transitioned to human. Smaller donor numbers; supports taphonomic experiments.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Cool/windy/low-elevation/acidic soil/poor drainage + vulture effects on decomposition rates/patterns vs. other US sites; clandestine disposal; postmortem alterations.
Climate/Environment Notes: Cool temperate continental; windy, acidic soils, variable seasons
Unique Features/Notes: Comparative taphonomy across climates; vulture scavenging parallels Texas. Forensic case consultations + LE training. Acidic soil unique for bone preservation.
Other Helpful Data: Important for regional variation understanding; started with animal proxies for ethics/scale.

History of Directors: Founding/early leadership under CMU Forensic Investigation program; Melissa Connor key
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: 11+ human cadavers by 2018; ongoing for arid research. Limited public aggregate metrics.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: High-desert semi-arid decomposition; desiccation/mummification; PMI for desiccated remains; indoor/outdoor comparison; camera monitoring.
Size/Acreage: ~2 acres outdoor + indoor facilities (classroom, wet lab/morgue, cooler)
Climate/Environment Notes: High-desert semi-arid; low rainfall (~8.6 in/yr), high altitude (~4,750 ft), extreme temp swings, low humidity
Unique Features/Notes: Focus on desiccation/preservation in arid conditions (contrasts humid sites). Indoor controlled studies. LE/student training. Early 'six body farms' group.
Other Helpful Data: Key for arid climate data; complements humid/semi-arid Texas and others. Strong visual documentation.

Facility Name: Facility for Outdoor Research and Training (FORT / USF-FORT)
Current Director: USF IFAAS / Anthropology faculty (e.g., Erin Kimmerle historically)
History of Directors: USF Florida Institute for Forensic Anthropology & Applied Sciences leadership
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Supports subtropical research; active donor program but limited public metrics.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Subtropical high-humidity decomposition; rapid insect/vegetation effects; PMI in Florida conditions; LE training.
Size/Acreage: Secure outdoor facility (limited public acreage details)
Climate/Environment Notes: Subtropical / humid; hot, rainy, high biodiversity/insects
Unique Features/Notes: Southernmost US facility; unique for rapid decomposition + vegetation. Fills Southeast US/Caribbean analog gap. Training-focused alongside research.
Other Helpful Data: Part of 'seven recognized U.S. body farms'. Important for extreme humid/subtropical data.

Facility Name: Human Taphonomy Research Site / FROST (Forensic Research Outdoor Station)
Associated University/Entity: Northern Michigan University (NMU Center for Forensic Science)
Current Director: Dr. Jane Wankmiller (Director of FROST / Center for Forensic Science)
History of Directors: Founding under NMU Forensic Science program; Dr. Jane Wankmiller key/current
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Permanent curation ('Forever Wildcat' program). Focus on cold-climate research; limited public aggregate metrics.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: World's first cold/boreal climate decomposition; snow/freeze-thaw cycles; low-temp effects; interdisciplinary (archaeology, soil science, climatology, entomology); LE/medicolegal training.
Climate/Environment Notes: Cold boreal / subarctic transitional; heavy snow, freeze-thaw, short summers, coniferous forest
Unique Features/Notes: Northernmost US facility; critical for northern latitude/winter data gaps. Strong undergrad research. Ethical permanent donation model. Interdisciplinary collaborations.
Other Helpful Data: Fills major global north climate gap. Part of NMU Center for Forensic Science with FARL lab.

Facility Name: Forensic Science Research and Training Laboratory (FSRTL)
Associated University/Entity: George Mason University (GMU Forensic Science Program)
Current Director: Mary Ellen O'Toole (Director, GMU Forensic Science Program)
History of Directors: New facility under GMU College of Science Forensic Science Program (O'Toole key)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: New; small initial donations (2+ in 2024). Focus on Mid-Atlantic/Northeast variation. Documented for research.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Geographic taphonomy variation for Mid-Atlantic (temperate/seasonal/urban-adjacent); 3D documentation/imaging (first FARO-certified lab); crime scene processing; outdoor training; emerging tech applications.
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate Mid-Atlantic; four seasons, moderate humidity, mixed forest/urban interface
Unique Features/Notes: Only human taphonomic facility in Mid-Atlantic/Northeast US. Strong tech integration (3D/FARO); local LE partnerships (Fairfax, GMU Police, Army). Training in crime scene house + outdoor lab. Newest major US addition.
Other Helpful Data: Fills critical regional gap; leverages DC metro proximity for federal/local agency training. Practical forensic applications focus.

Facility Name: Forensic Taphonomy Education and Research Facility (FTERF)
Associated University/Entity: Louisiana State University (LSU FACES Laboratory) + Louisiana State Police
Current Director: Megan E. Carroll, MIS (FTERF Manager); LSU FACES leadership (Ginesse Listi et al.; Mary Manhein founded FACES)
History of Directors: Newer; LSU FACES Lab (est. 1990 by Mary Manhein) oversees; Megan Carroll manager
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Prefers living/next-of-kin; unclaimed/indigent case-by-case with ethics review. All placed at FTERF then curated in LSU FACES Skeletal Collection.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Louisiana environment decomposition; natural progression observation; supports forensic casework, missing persons ID, training. FACES publications on anthropology/imaging/recovery.
Climate/Environment Notes: Humid subtropical (Louisiana bayou/forest; hot, very humid, high rainfall, dense vegetation/insects)
Unique Features/Notes: University + state police partnership model. Strong ethics/structural violence awareness in donations. After decomp, remains curated for skeletal collection + case support. Missing persons focus (Louisiana Repository).
Other Helpful Data: Newer Gulf Coast/subtropical addition. Ethical focus statement. Complements USF FORT.

Facility Name: Australian Facility for Taphonomic Experimental Research (AFTER)
Associated University/Entity: University of Technology Sydney (UTS Centre for Forensic Science)
Location (City, State/Province, Country): Near Yarramundi, New South Wales, Australia
Current Director: Shari Forbes, PhD (founder; key leadership; now also Canadian facility)
History of Directors: Shari Forbes (founder/primary 2016+); ongoing UTS Centre for Forensic Science team
Donor Packet/Info URL: Contact UTS Centre for Forensic Science or Shari Forbes (Australia-specific program)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Active donor program; supports bushfire victim recovery research. Unique Australian demographics/context.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Australian bushland/eucalypt decomposition; bushfire victim recovery/ID challenges; controlled outdoor taphonomy; Australian fauna scavenging, soil, climate effects. PMI in Australia, fire-affected remains, DVI.
Size/Acreage: Secure outdoor bushland facility (functional size, not publicly detailed)
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate to subtropical Australian bush; variable rainfall, eucalypt vegetation, unique fauna (kangaroos etc.), wildfire risk
Unique Features/Notes: First non-US body farm. Addresses Southern Hemisphere/Australian-specific conditions (bushfires, different insects/scavengers). Strong DVI/wildfire forensics. Shari Forbes pioneer (also Canadian facility).
Contact Info: UTS Centre for Forensic Science or via publications
Other Helpful Data: Pioneering international facility; enables comparative global taphonomy. Critical for Australia/NZ/Asia-Pacific forensics.

Facility Name: Secure Site for Research in Thanatology (SSRT / REST[ES])
Associated University/Entity: University of Quebec at Trois-Rivières (UQTR) / Shari Forbes research group
History of Directors: Shari Forbes (primary; expanded from Australia to Canada)
Main Website URL: https://www.uqtr.ca/ (search UQTR Shari Forbes taphonomy / REST[ES])
Donor Packet/Info URL: Contact UQTR or Shari Forbes group (Canadian donation program)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Canadian-specific; signed donor forms or next-of-kin. Supports cold-temperate research; smaller scale initially.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: Canadian temperate/continental decomposition (cold winters, snow, freeze-thaw); thanatology focus; buried/surface comparisons; telemetry/remote sensing. Canadian taphonomy, PMI in northern climates, DVI.
Size/Acreage: Secure outdoor research site (functional size, not publicly detailed)
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate continental / cold winters; snow cover, freeze-thaw cycles, boreal influences
Unique Features/Notes: Canada's dedicated human taphonomy facility; fills North American northern gap alongside NMU. Shari Forbes' second major facility. Focus on thanatology, ethics, practical Canadian forensics (remote areas, winter recovery).
Contact Info: UQTR or Shari Forbes via publications
Other Helpful Data: Important for global north climate data; international collaboration potential with US/Canada/Australia network.

Facility Name: Amsterdam Research Initiative for Sub-surface Taphonomy and Anthropology (ARISTA)
Associated University/Entity: Amsterdam University Medical Centers (Amsterdam UMC, Academic Medical Center)
Date Opened/Established: First inhumation March 2018; officially opened Nov 2018
Current Director: R.J. Oostra, PhD and team (founders/leads; Amsterdam UMC forensic anthropology/pathology)
History of Directors: R.J. Oostra et al. (2020 paper on realization); pioneering European team
Donor Packet/Info URL: Contact Amsterdam UMC Forensic Medicine or ARISTA leads (European body-donation authorized for taphonomic research)
Donor Metrics (Numbers, Demographics if: Europe's first; dedicated body-donation program for research. Emerging metrics; supports buried body studies. European demographics/context.
Key Published Studies/Research Focus: First dedicated HTF in Europe; systematic buried human remains under known conditions; telemetry, remote sensing, geophysics for detection; temperate maritime decomposition; soil interactions, preservation. Oostra et al. 2020+ papers.
Size/Acreage: Secure facility focused on inhumations (buried); specialized size
Climate/Environment Notes: Temperate maritime (mild, wet, seasonal; moderate temps, high humidity, clay/peat soils)
Unique Features/Notes: Europe's pioneer HTF; buried-body emphasis (vs. surface in most US). Addresses European legal/ethical/cultural contexts. Remote sensing for clandestine graves (cold cases, humanitarian). Blueprint for pan-European network (UNDER group).
Contact Info: Amsterdam UMC Forensic Dept or via 2020 Forensic Sci Int paper
Other Helpful Data: Critical for Europe/UK/global buried taphonomy data; contrasts surface-focused US facilities. Enables international comparisons. Ethical model for new facilities.
